summary: It's been a wild day around the BeltLine! Construction's finally starting on that Reynoldstown townhome project that's been sitting empty for years, and there's a new Asian fusion spot opening in Inman Park. Meanwhile, Atlanta's dealing with major airport chaos as TSA agents work without pay.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown BeltLine Corner Development Finally Breaks Ground
topStory.summary: That cleared lot on Chester Avenue that's been sitting empty for five years is finally seeing action. Embry Development is moving forward with 85 townhomes and condos just a block from the Eastside Trail. It's one of those projects we've all been watching and wondering if it would ever happen.
topStory.body:
You know that corner at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace in Reynoldstown that's been an empty lot since they cleared the old buildings back in 2021? Well, construction equipment is finally rolling in. Embry Development Company took over from Toll Brothers a couple years back and now they're actually moving dirt on what they're calling Camber Crossing.

This is 85 residential units we're talking about, townhomes and for-sale condos, sitting just a block west of the BeltLine's Eastside Trail and about a block north of Memorial Drive. The location is pretty sweet if you think about it - walking distance to the trail, close enough to bike to Krog Street Market or East Atlanta Village. It's exactly the kind of missing middle housing the area needs, especially with everything else getting more expensive around the BeltLine.

The timing feels right too. The BeltLine corridor has been filling in steadily, and Reynoldstown has been one of the neighborhoods that's seen steady but not overwhelming change. This project has been permitted and ready to go for years, so it's nice to see shovels finally in the ground instead of just more speculation about what might happen.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction beginning at Camber Crossing development site in Reynoldstown

stories.imageAlt: Construction beginning at Camber Crossing development site in Reynoldstown

stories.headline: Decatur's New Village-Style Housing Offers ITP Alternative
stories.summary: Cottages at Midway is getting ready to soft-launch next week on Midway Road. It's 18 standalone houses built around courtyards and greenspace, designed to be more accessible than Decatur's $700k+ median home prices. The kind of missing middle housing we need more of.
stories.body:
With Decatur home prices hitting over $700,000, finding anything affordable ITP feels impossible. But Cottages at Midway on Midway Road might offer some hope. This mixed-income community is putting 18 standalone houses on 2.1 acres of formerly underused lots, and they're doing it village-style with shared courtyards and greenspace.

The soft launch starts next week, which means they'll be testing the waters to see how much demand there is for this kind of development. It's the missing middle housing that everybody talks about but rarely gets built - not apartments, not McMansions, but actual houses that working families might be able to afford near a major commercial corridor.

What makes this interesting is the location and design. You're close to downtown Decatur but not paying downtown Decatur prices (hopefully). The shared courtyard concept feels more community-oriented than your typical subdivision, and with Decatur's walkability and transit access, it could be a model for how to add density without losing neighborhood character.
stories.imageAlt: Site map of Cottages at Midway development in Decatur

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Opens in Inman Park with Peking Chicken and Craft Cocktails
stories.summary: Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez just opened their first restaurant on Elizabeth Street, right off the Freedom Park Trail. They're serving Asian-American comfort food like soft shell crab bao and Thai tea martinis. After years of pop-ups, they finally have their own spot.
stories.body:
After years of working for other people and running pop-ups, chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez finally have their own place. Mushi Ni opened today on Elizabeth Street in Inman Park, just off the Freedom Park Trail, and it's exactly the kind of spot the neighborhood needed.

The menu is Asian-American comfort food that actually sounds comforting - soft shell crab bao, chicken longanisa bao, umami fries, and their signature Peking chicken platter that feeds two people for $25. Le worked at places like Staplehouse, and Jimenez has serious NYC experience including working for Gordon Ramsay, so they know what they're doing. The cocktail program includes Thai tea martinis and tamarind whiskey sours, which sounds way more interesting than another basic craft cocktail menu.

What I love about this is the location and the approach. Elizabeth Street has been quietly becoming a little food corridor, and being right off the Freedom Park Trail means you can bike or walk there from a bunch of neighborhoods. Plus, it's nice to see experienced chefs finally getting to do their own thing instead of just working for other people's concepts. The price points seem reasonable too, especially for Inman Park.
stories.imageAlt: Peking chicken dish at Mushi Ni restaurant

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue Brings Austin-Style Brisket to the BeltLine
stories.summary: John Lewis, the pitmaster who helped put Austin barbecue on the map, just opened at Ansley Mall behind The Cook's Warehouse. The smokehouse upstairs has panoramic windows overlooking the new BeltLine bridge, and they're turning out some of the best brisket in Atlanta.
stories.body:
The smokehouse at Lewis Barbecue is unlike anything I've seen in Atlanta. Most places hide their smokers in back rooms or outbuildings, but John Lewis put his upstairs in a loft with floor-to-ceiling windows overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the BeltLine. Six 22-foot smokers that Lewis helped weld from 1,000-gallon propane tanks run 24 hours a day in eight-hour shifts, looking like the fanciest engine room in Georgia.

Lewis made his name in Charleston and Austin, and now he's bringing that experience to Ansley Mall. The brisket is the real deal - properly smoked, great bark, and that perfect balance of smoke and beef flavor. The location works perfectly too. You can walk over from the BeltLine, grab some barbecue, and head back to Piedmont Park or wherever you're going.

What makes this special is the attention to detail and the commitment to the craft. Lewis didn't just phone this in or slap his name on someone else's operation. He's running a serious barbecue program in a space that celebrates the smoking process instead of hiding it. Plus, being right on the BeltLine corridor means it's accessible to people from all over the city, not just folks with cars.
stories.imageAlt: Barbecue platter at Lewis Barbecue with brisket and sides

stories.headline: Robert Montwaid's New Eastside Food Hall Opens in April
stories.summary: The guy who created NYC's Gansevoort Market is opening Vela Park Food Works near East Lake Golf Club in mid-April. It's his third Atlanta food hall, with six chef-driven concepts, craft cocktails, and outdoor dining at the new Vela Park rental community.
stories.body:
Robert Montwaid knows how to create a food destination. After founding NYC's famous Gansevoort Market and opening successful food halls here in Atlanta, he's bringing his expertise to the eastside with Vela Park Food Works. The food hall is finishing construction now at the new Vela Park rental community near East Lake Golf Club, with a mid-April opening planned.

This will be six different chef-driven concepts under one roof, plus craft cocktails and what sounds like substantial outdoor dining space. Montwaid has proven he can curate interesting food vendors and create spaces that feel like more than just mall food courts, so expectations are pretty high for this one.

The location is interesting too. East Lake isn't exactly a food destination right now, but it's got good MARTA access and it's close enough to Decatur, East Atlanta, and Kirkwood that people will make the trip if the food is good enough. Plus, being part of a new residential development means they'll have a built-in customer base, which always helps a food hall get established.
stories.imageAlt: Interior rendering of Vela Park Food Works food hall

stories.headline: Decatur's West Howard Complete Streets Makeover Nearly Done
stories.summary: After nearly a decade of planning, Decatur's transformation of West Howard Avenue from a drag strip into a people-friendly Complete Street is almost finished. The project includes protected bike lanes, new sidewalks, and traffic calming from Commerce Drive to the city limits at East Lake MARTA.
stories.body:
West Howard Avenue through Decatur has been a high-speed nightmare for pedestrians and cyclists for years, but that's finally changing. The Complete Streets project that kicked off last August is nearly done, transforming about a mile of roadway from Commerce Drive near Decatur High all the way to the city limits at East Lake MARTA station.

The transformation is dramatic - you've got a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers for most of the route, new sidewalks, repaved streets, and actual infrastructure that acknowledges people might want to get around without a car. This has been in the works for almost a decade, so it's great to see it actually happening instead of just being talked about in meetings.

What makes this significant is the connectivity it creates. Now you can safely bike or walk from downtown Decatur to East Lake MARTA and beyond, which opens up a lot of possibilities for car-free trips. It's also a good example of what Complete Streets can look like when they're done right - not just painted bike lanes that disappear at intersections, but actual protected infrastructure that people of all ages might actually use.
stories.imageAlt: New protected bike lanes on West Howard Avenue in Decatur

stories.headline: ICE Agents Deploy at Hartsfield-Jackson as Airport Chaos Continues
stories.summary: With TSA agents working without pay and massive security lines, the Trump administration sent ICE agents to help at the world's busiest airport. They're not doing immigration enforcement, just providing security presence as the partial government shutdown drags on.
stories.body:
The scenes at Hartsfield-Jackson have been pretty wild this week. With TSA agents working without pay since the partial government shutdown began in mid-February, security lines stretched for hours and thousands of agents called out sick. So the federal government sent in ICE agents in tactical gear to help provide security presence, though they're not involved in actual security screening or immigration enforcement.

Travelers are being told to arrive four hours early, and some people are spending entire nights in security lines. It's creating exactly the kind of chaos you'd expect when you don't pay essential workers for weeks on end. The ICE deployment is meant to provide extra security while TSA tries to keep operations running with skeleton crews.

Mayor Dickens issued a statement assuring everyone that the ICE agents won't be doing immigration enforcement during this deployment, which is important given Atlanta's immigrant communities. But the whole situation just shows how badly the shutdown is affecting basic government services. When the world's busiest airport can't function normally, that's a pretty clear sign that something's broken at the federal level.
stories.imageAlt: ICE agents at Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport

stories.headline: 7-Year-Old Dies in Accidental Shooting in DeKalb County
stories.summary: A tragic accident on Loveless Drive in the Ellenwood area overnight. Family members say the child was playing with a firearm when it discharged. Police are investigating what they're calling an 'non-intended discharge of a weapon.'
stories.body:
This is the kind of story that just breaks your heart. DeKalb County police responded to a home on Loveless Drive in the Ellenwood area during the early morning hours and found a 7-year-old boy dead inside. Family members at the scene told reporters the child was playing with a firearm when it accidentally discharged.

Police are investigating this as what they're calling a 'non-intended discharge of a weapon,' which is their way of saying accidental shooting. The road was blocked off for hours as investigators worked the scene, and you could see the grief and shock in the faces of family and friends who gathered in the neighborhood.

These accidents are preventable tragedies that happen way too often. Gun safety in homes with children is something every gun owner needs to take seriously - secure storage, trigger locks, keeping firearms unloaded and inaccessible. One moment of unsecured storage and a family's life is changed forever. My heart goes out to this family dealing with an unimaginable loss.
stories.imageAlt: DeKalb County police investigating scene of accidental shooting

stories.headline: Atlanta City Council Advances ICE Controls, Questions Fire Union Contract
stories.summary: The Public Safety Committee unanimously moved forward resolutions barring ICE detention facilities in the city and requiring APD to document ICE activities. Meanwhile, dozens of residents criticized Mayor Dickens for not signing the firefighters' union contract that council already ratified.
stories.body:
The Public Safety Committee meeting Monday was packed with residents pushing for two very different issues. First, they unanimously advanced resolutions that would ban ICE detention facilities within Atlanta city limits and require Atlanta police to document when ICE agents are present during their operations. The full council votes on both measures April 20.

But the bigger drama was around the firefighters' union contract. Dozens of Atlantans showed up criticizing Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign the city's first-ever contract with the firefighters' union, even though city council ratified it back in April 2025. This is a pretty unusual situation - council approved it, but the mayor won't sign it, leaving the firefighters in limbo.

The ICE resolutions feel particularly relevant given what's happening at the airport right now with federal agents being deployed there. Atlanta has always positioned itself as a welcoming city, and these measures would formalize that stance. The fire union situation, though, is more about basic labor relations and whether the mayor can just ignore what council has already approved.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta City Council Public Safety Committee meeting

stories.headline: DeKalb Parents Pack School Closure Meetings with Frustration
stories.summary: Hundreds of angry parents showed up at Lakeside High for the latest round of DeKalb County Schools closure discussions. The protests outside Oak Grove and Evansdale families made it clear they don't think the district is actually listening to community input on the Student Assignment Project.
stories.body:
The scene outside Lakeside High on March 23 told the whole story. Parents and kids from Oak Grove and Evansdale elementary schools were holding signs demanding a student-first approach to school districting, clearly frustrated with how DeKalb County Schools is handling the controversial Student Assignment Project.

'This is not a process. This is a farce, and they're not listening,' said Samuel Howe, an Oak Grove parent. That sentiment was echoed by families who feel like the district is just going through the motions of community input while having already made up their minds about which schools to close or reorganize.

The Student Assignment Project affects schools throughout DeKalb, but the pushback from these specific elementary school communities shows how personal these decisions are for families. When you're talking about closing neighborhood schools or completely redrawing attendance zones, you're messing with communities that have built their lives around those schools. The fact that parents are taking to the streets says everything about how this process has been handled.
stories.imageAlt: Parents and children protesting DeKalb school closures outside Lakeside High
